page: Amazon Web Services r4.2xlarge is a memory-optimized fourth-generation instance. It is built on the x86_64 architecture and powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. The instance features 8 dedicated vCPUs derived from 4 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It is equipped with 61.0 GB of system memory, providing 7.62 GB per core, and a 45 MB L3 cache. It operates on the Xen hypervisor, includes a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps, and does not feature local storage or GPU accelerators.

Benchmark results indicate that the CPU performance of the r4.2xlarge is relatively modest. Single-core processing speeds, as measured by stress-ng and Geekbench, fall into the bottom 10% of tested servers, while multi-core performance sits in the bottom 25%. Memory bandwidth tests show a split profile: read-only operations on large, potentially uncached blocks achieve average performance in the middle 50%, whereas small-block cached reads and large-block writes fall into the bottom 25%. Database operations, such as Redis SET commands, perform in the bottom 10%.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio without the premium of newer generation hardware, making it a viable option for memory-intensive legacy workloads that do not require high-speed compute. However, the tradeoff is lower processing throughput and the lack of local NVMe storage. The r4.2xlarge is best suited for memory-bound workloads such as medium-sized databases, caching layers, and basic data processing tasks where memory capacity is prioritized over raw CPU speed.
description: Amazon Web Services r4.2xlarge is a memory-optimized, fourth-generation instance designed for workloads requiring a high memory footprint relative to compute resources. Powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor on the x86_64 architecture, it provides 8 dedicated vCPUs across 4 physical cores, 61.0 GB of RAM, and a 2 Gbps baseline network bandwidth, with no local storage or GPUs. Benchmark data shows modest CPU performance, with single-core and multi-core results falling into the bottom 10% and 25% respectively. However, its uncached large-block memory read bandwidth achieves average performance. This resource profile offers qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy legacy applications that do not demand high compute performance. The r4.2xlarge is suited for memory-bound workloads such as caching layers, in-memory databases, and general data processing.
